items.question: Who needs to distinguish convex from non-convex optimization?
items.answer: ML engineers choosing model classes, researchers analyzing training dynamics, and anyone interpreting whether gradient descent guarantees a global optimum or only a local one.

items.question: What makes a function convex?
items.answer: The chord property f(t x + (1-t) y) <= t f(x) + (1-t) f(y) for t in [0,1]; for smooth f on R^n, equivalently the Hessian is positive semidefinite everywhere.

items.question: When does local = global?
items.answer: When minimizing a convex function over a convex domain: any local minimum is also a global minimum, so first-order methods cannot be trapped in a suboptimal valley.

items.question: Where do saddle points appear?
items.answer: At critical points (gradient zero) where the Hessian is indefinite—common on neural loss surfaces and on functions like f(x,y)=x^2-y^2 at the origin.

items.question: Why train convex models first?
items.answer: Convex baselines (linear/logistic regression) give certifiable global optima and isolate whether poor performance comes from model capacity or optimization failure.

items.question: How do you classify a critical point in two variables?
items.answer: Compute the Hessian H at the point; if all eigenvalues > 0 it is a local minimum, all < 0 a local maximum, mixed signs a saddle.

items.question: Convex vs non-convex loss: what changes for gradient descent?
items.answer: Convex: any local minimum is global; descent is structurally reliable with proper step size. Non-convex: multiple minima and saddles exist; the final weights depend on initialization, noise, and schedule—same optimizer, weaker guarantees.

items.question: Local minimum vs saddle point: how do they differ?
items.answer: Local minimum: f increases in every small neighborhood (Hessian positive definite in smooth settings). Saddle: gradient zero but you can decrease f along some directions (Hessian indefinite)—looks like progress stopped even though better points exist nearby.
